items.title: Warsh Day 1: easing bias gone, ~50% FOMC expects hike by year-end
items.idea: Kevin Warsh chaired his first FOMC meeting and held rates at 3.5–3.75% but removed the easing bias — nearly half the committee now projects a hike before year-end. CPI is running at 4.2% y/y (3-year high), driven by an energy spike. Crucially, Warsh abstained from submitting a dot to the SEP, signaling a structural change to how the Fed communicates forecasts. His explicit goal: act less, telegraph less, treat market surprise as a feature not a bug.
items.angle: This is the most significant Fed regime change since Volcker. The market priced 'hold' — it didn't price 'hike bias plus opacity.' Long-end resilience vs short-end selling is THE chart to watch here.
items.tweetA: Warsh Day 1: removes easing bias, abstains from the dot plot, and signals he won't telegraph moves in advance. The Fed just went from being a babysitter to being a grown-up. Markets haven't fully priced what that means for risk premia.
items.tweetB: CPI at 4.2% y/y. Half the FOMC now expects a hike. And the new Chair says he won't give you hints before he acts. The last 15 years trained every portfolio manager to front-run the Fed. That trade is now structurally broken.

charts.desc: Dot plot showing hike expectations shift

items.title: Warsh kills forward guidance: 'no more bedtime stories' about next cut
items.idea: New Fed Chair Warsh explicitly told markets he will stop providing forward guidance — no more hints about what the next meeting will do. He will simply announce decisions on the day. Blocmates notes this creates a 30% probability of a July rate hike now being priced. This is a fundamental change in how institutional risk management works: you can no longer hedge using Fed signals.
items.angle: Every rate-vol trader and macro hedge fund has built models around Fed communication patterns. Those models break if the Fed goes silent. Vol should reprice across the entire rates curve.
items.tweetA: Warsh just told markets: I'm not giving you a bedtime story about the next cut. The Fed's entire communication apparatus — dots, press conferences, minutes — was designed to reduce uncertainty. He just threw it in the bin. Vol surfaces need to reset.
items.tweetB: 30% chance of a July hike is now in the market. But that's almost beside the point. The real shift is structural: we've had 15 years of a 'transparent Fed.' That era is over. Price accordingly.

items.title: Investment quadrant: 'Speculation Phase' — equities lower, commodities up, bonds problematic
items.idea: Capital Wars argues the current environment fits their 'Speculation Phase' of the investment quadrant: equities deliver lower returns with higher volatility, long-dated bonds often struggle, commodities outperform, and the real economy gains traction as inflation concerns build. After the first Warsh FOMC, the yield curve is seeing bearish flattening. The long end proved more resilient than the short end post-meeting — they read this as a positive signal for bonds relative to equity.
items.angle: The investment quadrant framework gives a systematic way to position. If we're truly in 'Speculation,' the commodity overweight and equity underweight is the trade — not chasing AI multiple expansion.
items.tweetA: Capital Wars: we're in the 'Speculation Phase' of the investment cycle. Historically this means: equities disappoint, commodities outperform, yield curve flattens bearishly. After Warsh's first meeting, long bonds held better than the front end. That's the signal they're watching.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching equities. Capital Wars says the real tell is the 10yr vs 2yr move after Wednesday's FOMC. Long end resilience relative to front end skyrocketing = the bond market liked Warsh's message. Now the question is whether equities catch up to that repricing.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: 10yr vs 2yr spread showing bearish flattening post-Warsh FOMC

items.title: $8.3 trillion June options expiration today — 18% larger than previous all-time record
items.idea: Today marks the June options expiration with ~$8.3 trillion of US options exposure rolling off — 18% larger than the previous record set in December (~$7.1 trillion). This is happening simultaneously with the market rallying on the Iran deal and processing a hawkish Fed pivot. Options expirations of this magnitude create extreme gamma effects and forced dealer hedging flows that can dramatically amplify or suppress intraday moves.
items.angle: The combo of: record opex + Warsh hawkish shock + Iran relief rally = maximum mechanical chaos. Whoever is short gamma here is in pain.
items.tweetA: $8.3 trillion in options exposure rolling off today. That's 18% bigger than the previous record set in December. Add in: Warsh's hawkish pivot, the Iran peace deal, and 2-year yields at 16-month highs. This is what maximum mechanical chaos looks like.
items.tweetB: The largest options expiration in history just happened. $8.3 trillion. And yet stocks are rallying because of an Iran peace deal. This is the kind of day where whatever the market does intraday means almost nothing — and everything that settles after the close means a lot.

items.title: BOJ raised rates to levels not seen since 1995; USD/JPY above 160; intervention on the table
items.idea: The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate this week to levels not seen since 1995 — yet USD/JPY still sits above 160. The rate hike provided zero relief for the yen, which is being overwhelmed by Fed hawkishness and dollar strength. Japanese authorities are explicitly warning that intervention is 'absolutely on the table.' BofA forecasts further BoJ hikes to 1.75% by end-2027. The MSCI Asia index hit a new all-time high up +7% over 5 sessions.
items.angle: The Japan carry trade is the biggest macro time bomb nobody is talking about. Capital Flows did a livestream on it today. If the yen weakens further toward 165, the unwind risk becomes systemic — everyone who is short yen to fund long everything else.
items.tweetA: Japan just raised rates to the highest since 1995. USD/JPY went above 160 anyway. Japanese authorities are saying intervention is 'absolutely on the table.' The BoJ is tightening into a yen that doesn't care. This is the carry trade stress signal.
items.tweetB: The Nikkei and Kospi hit all-time highs today. The MSCI Asia index +7% in 5 sessions. And yet USD/JPY is at 160+ despite a BoJ rate hike. Either Asia equities are right or the yen move is right. They can't both be right.

charts.desc: USD/JPY chart and BoJ rate path

items.title: BOE holds at 3.75% with two dissenters; UK youth unemployment at decade high
items.idea: The Bank of England held rates at 3.75% but two of nine MPC members voted for an immediate hike to 4% (Megan Greene and Huw Pill). Governor Bailey said falling oil prices are 'encouraging' but warned of second-round inflation risks. Meanwhile UK data is grim: youth unemployment at 16.2% — highest in over a decade — private sector pay growth at weakest since 2020, job vacancies at lowest since spring 2021, and the UK faces political turbulence with Andy Burnham expected to challenge Starmer for Labour leadership.
items.angle: The UK is simultaneously dealing with inflation risk and labor market weakness — the classic stagflation trap. And now a political leadership contest. Sterling is weakest since early April. $GBP short looks compelling.
items.tweetA: BOE holds but two members voted to hike. Meanwhile UK youth unemployment just hit its highest level in over a decade at 16.2%. Private sector pay growth: weakest since 2020. Job vacancies: lowest since spring 2021. This isn't a rate problem. It's a structural problem.
items.tweetB: The UK has: 16.2% youth unemployment (decade high), private sector wages at 2.9% growth (lowest since 2020), and a political civil war coming as Burnham challenges Starmer. Sterling at lowest since April. The BOE is in an impossible position.

items.title: Trump signs Iran peace deal at Versailles; Hormuz reopening; Brent slides to $78
items.idea: Trump formally signed an interim peace deal with Iran at the Palace of Versailles on Wednesday evening. The Strait of Hormuz is set to reopen over a complex 60-day negotiating period on Iran's nuclear program. Trump said further military conflict 'could have caused an international depression.' Brent crude slid 1.5% toward $78 — its 6th consecutive down session — as markets priced the supply return. However, Israeli forces launched fresh airstrikes in Lebanon Thursday morning, threatening the fragile pact.
items.angle: The Hormuz reopening is deflationary for energy — but the Lebanon wildcard is real. If the truce fractures, oil rips back. The energy trade for the next 60 days is about monitoring Israeli escalation, not the Iran deal itself.
items.tweetA: Iran peace deal signed at Versailles. Strait of Hormuz reopening. Brent crude down 6 sessions in a row to $78. But: Israel launched fresh airstrikes in Lebanon this morning — the same peace Trump celebrated yesterday. The next 60 days are about Lebanon, not Iran.
items.tweetB: Trump says abandoning the Iran talks 'could have caused an international depression.' That's an admission of how close we got to a $150 oil scenario. The deal is fragile. Israel just struck Lebanon. If this unravels, the oil move back up will be violent.

items.title: Ukraine drones hit Moscow Oil Refinery twice this week; 194 drones downed; airports closed
items.idea: Ukraine launched a record air bombardment on Moscow overnight — at least 194 drones were downed over the city. Some drones reached the Moscow Oil Refinery (a key supplier of road fuel to Moscow and surrounding areas), which is the second strike on the refinery this week after Tuesday's attack set it ablaze. Operations were suspended at all four of Moscow's passenger airports. In response, Putin has intensified strikes on Kyiv. This escalation comes simultaneously with the Iran peace deal.
items.angle: An attack on Moscow's fuel supply infrastructure is qualitatively different from Ukraine hitting military targets. This changes the domestic Russian political calculus — and could influence energy markets if the refinery damage compounds.
items.tweetA: Ukraine hit the Moscow Oil Refinery twice this week. 194 drones downed over Moscow overnight. All four Moscow airports suspended. This isn't a symbolic strike — it's attacking Russia's capital fuel supply. The domestic Russian political pressure from this is underappreciated.
items.tweetB: The Iran peace deal made headlines. Buried: Ukraine just conducted the largest drone attack ever on Moscow, hitting the city's main oil refinery for the second time in 48 hours. The peace in the Middle East and the escalation in Ukraine are happening simultaneously.

items.title: G7 + EU push to diversify from China; Colombia runoff Sunday with binary outcome
items.idea: The G7 this week pledged to diversify away from China, adding momentum to EU trade talks with Beijing. France's Macron warned of 'strong measures' including potential tariffs if China doesn't address the trade imbalance. The EU's trade negotiator said the relationship with China is 'simply not sustainable.' Separately, Colombia holds a runoff presidential election Sunday — right-wing lawyer Abelardo De La Espriella vs far-left socialist Ivan Cepeda, a binary outcome with major implications for Colombian equities.
items.angle: Two different geopolitical pivots on the same day. The China diversification story is slow-moving but gigantic — re-shoring, supply chain restructuring, and tariff risk. Colombia is a binary event play this weekend.
items.tweetA: G7 pledges to diversify from China. EU considers tariffs. Macron warns of 'strong measures.' Meanwhile China's BMW just issued a drastic profit warning due to China exposure. The G7 is finally saying what European CFOs have been experiencing for 18 months.
items.tweetB: Colombia votes Sunday — right-wing De La Espriella vs far-left socialist Ivan Cepeda. This is a binary event for Colombian equities. Far-left wins = resource nationalism risk, capital flight. Right-wing wins = reform, re-rating. Geiger Capital has active positions.

items.title: Debunking '50% of 2026 datacenter capacity canceled' — NA hyperscaler forecast moved only ~1%
items.idea: The viral claim that 'half of 2026 US datacenter capacity is canceled' traces back to a Bloomberg April 1 article about China-dependent electrical equipment. SemiAnalysis, which tracks every datacenter site individually, reports their NA Hyperscaler Self-build YE2026 forecast has moved only ~1% over the last 6 months, and NA colocation <5%. The 'vibecoded estimates' from secondary reporting are dramatically wrong — actual cancellations are minor vs. what's circulating on financial and social media.
items.angle: This matters enormously for the AI infrastructure trade. If people are shorting $EQIX $DLR $VRT $SMCI on cancellation fears that are 10x overstated, there's a massive position unwind coming when earnings prove the bears wrong.
items.tweetA: SemiAnalysis tracks every datacenter site. Their YE2026 NA hyperscaler self-build forecast has moved ~1% in 6 months. The viral claim that '50% of 2026 datacenter capacity is canceled' is financial media fiction. Don't vibecode your estimates — go through every filing. $EQIX $DLR $VRT
items.tweetB: Everyone's been short AI infrastructure on 'datacenter cancellation' fears. SemiAnalysis just showed the actual number: NA hyperscaler capacity forecast moved 1% in 6 months. Bloomberg's April 1 article spawned 10 clickbait rewrites and became consensus. The consensus is wrong.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: Actual vs rumored datacenter capacity forecast changes

items.title: AI agents execute corporate bond trades for first time (LTX/Broadridge)
items.idea: LTX, Broadridge's corporate bond trading platform, now allows users to create AI agents on its BondGPT app that can build trade tickets, select dealers, send RFQs, and auto-execute certain trades. This is the first case of AI agents performing agentic actions in the bond market. Unlike old-style automation (explicit rules), agentic AI lets you define a goal without specifying every step — which is particularly suited to bond markets where information infrastructure is fragmented across thousands of dealers and issuances.
items.angle: Bond markets are a $50T+ market that still runs largely on phone calls and manual RFQs. If AI agents compress the information disadvantage in bond price discovery, you're looking at a compression in bid-ask spreads that transfer wealth from dealers to buyside. $BEN $BLK watch this.
items.tweetA: AI agents can now execute corporate bond trades autonomously on LTX/Broadridge. First time ever. The bond market is 10x the size of equities and still runs on phone calls. The agents that figure out fragmented dealer pricing first will eat the spread that humans have charged for decades.
items.tweetB: Why does agentic AI matter for bonds specifically? Because bond markets have 50,000 different securities, no central exchange, and pricing that's historically opaque. That's exactly the environment where 'define a goal, not every step' AI outperforms rule-based automation. The dealer spread is the prize.

items.title: Token-based AI billing spreading: Anthropic → Google → now Microsoft Copilot
items.idea: Microsoft's Copilot coding product is moving to usage-based (token-based) billing, following Anthropic and Google. This forces enterprise software companies to evaluate their AI spending carefully — flat-rate subscriptions are being replaced by consumption models. TheStreet notes this could force companies to reassess reliance on AI tools, while the market reconciles how disruptive LLM technology really is when it becomes metered rather than all-you-can-eat.
items.angle: Token-based billing is a much stronger signal of AI utility than subscription growth. If enterprises continue paying as usage bills grow, that's the strongest possible demand signal. If they cap usage, that tells you AI isn't yet generating enough ROI. Watch $MSFT enterprise renewal rates.
items.tweetA: Anthropic moved to token billing. Then Google. Now Microsoft Copilot. The all-you-can-eat AI subscription era is ending. Consumption-based pricing is more honest — it forces ROI discipline on every enterprise. The companies that keep paying are the ones where AI actually works.
items.tweetB: The shift to token-based AI billing is actually bullish for the AI providers. It means customers pay for what they use — and as AI capabilities compound, usage naturally grows. The risk is that enterprises discover what the real cost of AI at scale looks like. That number is large.

items.title: Congress AI knowledge gap: 1,346 AI bills, $6M lobbying, no independent expertise
items.idea: This year alone, 48 US states have introduced 1,346 AI bills. Anthropic and OpenAI spent $6M on lobbying last year — but Meta spent $26M and Google $16.5M. In the absence of independent technical expertise in Congress (the OTA was abolished by Newt Gingrich in 1995 for $22M/year), company-sponsored briefings fill the vacuum — delivering messages aligned with commercial interests. Germany never abolished its OTA equivalent and published a parliamentary AI study within 6 months of ChatGPT's launch.
items.angle: The lobbying asymmetry (Big Tech $42M+ vs AI safety orgs almost nothing) means regulation will reflect Big Tech interests. The rebuilding of congressional AI expertise ($100M estimate for OTA equivalent) is a political trade-off that nobody wants to make before an election.
items.tweetA: 1,346 AI bills introduced in 48 states this year. Google spent $16.5M lobbying AI regulation. Anthropic + OpenAI spent $6M. The OTA — Congress's independent tech advisory body — was abolished in 1995 for costing $22M/year. Germany kept theirs. Germany had a parliamentary AI report 6 months after ChatGPT launched.
items.tweetB: The real AI governance crisis: the people writing laws for AI can't independently verify what the AI companies tell them. The OTA that could have helped was killed for $22M in savings. Today Anthropic alone spends $3M a year lobbying Congress. The vacuum isn't empty — it's filled by corporate interests.

items.title: Noam Shazeer joins OpenAI; MidJourney pivots to medical ultrasound
items.idea: Google Brain co-founder Noam Shazeer — widely credited as one of the key architects behind the Transformer architecture and Google's LLM capabilities — is joining OpenAI. Separately, MidJourney announced a new medical division called 'MidJourney Medical' and is launching a full-body ultrasound scanner. Both announcements signal major talent and product pivots in AI. Shazeer's move is arguably the most important AI talent event since Sam Altman's reinstatement.
items.angle: Shazeer at OpenAI is a massive capability signal. He built the architecture that powers almost every major LLM. If he's joining to build the next generation, the capability gap between OpenAI and competitors may widen significantly. The $GOOG reaction to this will be telling.
items.tweetA: Noam Shazeer — co-creator of the Transformer, one of the architects of everything that matters in LLMs — is joining OpenAI. This is the most significant AI talent move since Altman's reinstatement. The people who built Google's AI advantage are now at OpenAI. $GOOG
items.tweetB: MidJourney — known for text-to-image — just announced a full-body ultrasound scanner. The same week Noam Shazeer joins OpenAI. AI is no longer just a software story. It's a medical devices story. The category lines are dissolving faster than anyone expected.

items.title: Chief AI Officers: top banks hiring fast, but Citadel says role is 'short-lived'; regulators probe
items.idea: Bloomberg reports top financial firms are rushing to hire Chief AI Officers. But Citadel Securities CEO Peng Zhao said at a Hong Kong summit: 'We don't have a head of mobile devices' — implying the CAI-O is a transitional role. Meanwhile banking regulators are probing banks for their AI use policies, controls, and due diligence on AI vendors. AI Street expects this will evolve from enforcement of fraud cases to more nuanced policy-and-procedure enforcement as AI adoption scales.
items.angle: The CAI-O hiring wave is an AI adoption signal — but the Citadel framing is more interesting: what happens when AI is just infrastructure? The banks that get there fastest don't need a CAI-O because AI is in every team. The CAI-O is the transition cost.
items.tweetA: Banks are racing to hire Chief AI Officers. Citadel's Peng Zhao: 'We don't have a head of mobile devices.' He's right — the role exists because we're in transition, not because it's permanent. The banks that still have CAI-Os in 5 years lost the race.
items.tweetB: Banking regulators are now asking lenders: what AI are you using, what risks does it create, what controls do you have? This is step one. Step two is enforcement for answers that don't hold up. Step three is a $100M fine that becomes the industry's cautionary tale. The clock is ticking.

items.title: Bernie Sanders proposes sovereign wealth fund to publicly own AI companies
items.idea: Senator Bernie Sanders unveiled a plan to transfer public ownership of the largest AI companies via a US sovereign wealth fund. The proposal would shift profits from AI companies to public ownership rather than shareholders. This comes the same week Coinbase's AI agents were officially registered as SEC investment advisors — signaling both the left-wing political response to AI wealth concentration and the institutional legitimization of AI agents as financial actors.
items.angle: Sanders' proposal won't pass but it signals the political pressure building. The AI companies best positioned are those with demonstrable social benefit narratives. The worst positioned are pure-profit extraction models with no civic case. This is why Microsoft and Google are so focused on educational initiatives.
items.tweetA: Bernie Sanders just proposed a sovereign wealth fund to publicly own AI companies. This will go nowhere in the current Congress. But watch what happens in 3 years when AI-driven unemployment starts showing up in voting data. The political risk for AI companies is underpriced.
items.tweetB: The same week Sanders proposes nationalizing AI companies, Coinbase's AI agents become SEC-registered investment advisors. The political and regulatory system is reacting to AI faster than the AI companies expected. The window to self-regulate is closing.

items.title: Internet Capital Markets: SEC+CFTC classify Solana as digital commodity; JPM/Citi/State Street building on it
items.idea: Tiger Research argues crypto has crossed from experimentation into industry, taking the form of 'Internet Capital Markets' (ICM) — asset issuance, trading, and settlement all on a single public blockchain. In March 2026, the SEC and CFTC classified 16 assets including Solana as digital commodities. The GENIUS Act defined stablecoin legal status. J.P. Morgan, State Street, and Citi have built institutional cases on Solana. The Solana Policy Institute filed the Project Open pilot framework directly to the SEC — building precedent rather than waiting for it.
items.angle: If the jurisdictional race to define ICM standards plays out like the internet protocol wars of the 1990s, the chain that wins institutional settlement becomes the TCP/IP of finance. Solana's aggressive regulatory engagement strategy is the playbook. $SOL
items.tweetA: SEC and CFTC classified 16 assets including Solana as digital commodities in March 2026. JPMorgan, Citi, and State Street have all built institutional cases on Solana. The Solana Policy Institute filed directly with the SEC rather than waiting. This is what regulatory arbitrage looks like when it's done right. $SOL
items.tweetB: Tiger Research calls it 'Internet Capital Markets' — a structure where issuance, trading, and settlement happen on a single public blockchain. Capital markets still settle in T+1 through clearinghouses designed before the internet. The ICM eliminates that lag entirely. Solana is the lead candidate for the settlement layer.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: Institutional adoption cases on Solana

items.title: Backpack tokenized stocks: first truly real ownership, brokerage transfers — 74% DEX volume in days
items.idea: Backpack launched what the DeFi Investor calls the first 'real' tokenized US stocks and ETFs. Key differentiators vs existing products (Ondo, xStocks): (1) real ownership under New York law — survives Backpack bankruptcy, (2) same legal framework as Robinhood/Fidelity, (3) cash dividends, (4) brokerage transfers to Interactive Brokers, Robinhood — the first platform allowing this. In just days since launch, Backpack captured 74% of tokenized equities DEX volume. Coinbase also announced plans for 1:1 backed tokenized stocks. Citigroup plans to tokenize private company shares.
items.angle: The critical innovation is brokerage portability. Previous tokenized stocks were essentially IOUs — you couldn't take them off-chain. Once the mental block of 'why not just use Robinhood' is removed, you get the composability benefits of DeFi with the legal protection of TradFi. This is the missing link.
items.tweetA: Backpack just captured 74% of tokenized equity DEX volume within days of launch. Why? Because they solved the one question that killed every other tokenized stock product: 'Can I transfer this to Robinhood if I want?' The answer is now yes. That changes everything.
items.tweetB: Backpack tokenized stocks, Coinbase 1:1 tokenized stocks, Citigroup tokenizing private company shares — all in the same week. Tokenization has been 'coming' for 5 years. It's now arriving from every direction at once. The question shifts from 'will it happen' to 'which protocol captures the flow.'

charts.desc: Tokenized equity DEX volume market share chart

items.title: ETH RSI at lowest levels since TGE; Arthur Hayes buys 1,500 ETH
items.idea: Ethereum's RSI has fallen to its lowest levels since its token generation event (TGE) — historically these have been macro bottom signal areas. BTC is holding near $63K, which Blocmates flags as a critical level: closing below triggers risk of retesting $59-60K, while a bounce from here extends the potential run to $70-75K summer targets. Arthur Hayes publicly bought 1,500 ETH worth $2.63M. Andrew Tate was liquidated 8 times in 16 hours. Retail mania in stocks is at all-time records while crypto remains rangebound.
items.angle: The divergence between record retail equity mania and crypto underperformance is notable. Either crypto catches up (the classic alt-season setup) or retail mania in equities rolls over first. Hayes buying at the TGE RSI floor is either a legendary entry or a falling knife moment.
items.tweetA: ETH's RSI just hit the lowest levels since its token generation event. Arthur Hayes bought 1,500 ETH ($2.63M). Meanwhile retail stock mania is at all-time records while crypto sits rangebound. These are the conditions that historically precede either a major ETH bottom or an equity top. $ETH
items.tweetB: BTC at $63K is the line. Below it: retest of $59-60K. Above it: potential run to $70-75K. ETH RSI at TGE lows. Andrew Tate liquidated 8 times in 16 hours. The crypto market is in maximum pain for weak hands — which historically is when the setup gets interesting.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: ETH RSI chart showing TGE-level readings

items.title: Coinbase AI agents registered as SEC investment advisors — first time ever
items.idea: Coinbase's AI agents are now officially SEC-registered investment advisors. This is a regulatory first — an AI system receiving the same legal recognition as a human financial advisor. Simultaneously, AWS now allows content publishers to monetize AI bot traffic and receive USDC payments on Solana via the x402 protocol. These two developments together mark a moment where AI agents become first-class legal and economic actors, not just tools.
items.angle: When AI agents have SEC registration and can receive payment autonomously, you've crossed a threshold. These aren't copilots anymore. The liability and governance frameworks around AI agents are about to become the most important regulatory frontier in finance.
items.tweetA: Coinbase's AI agents are now SEC-registered investment advisors. An AI system has the same legal status as a human financial advisor. This is a quiet milestone that will seem obvious in retrospect and should be front-page news today.
items.tweetB: AWS now lets publishers monetize AI bot traffic for USDC on Solana. Coinbase's AI agents are SEC-registered advisors. AI agents are becoming economic actors with legal standing. The question of who is liable when an AI agent makes a bad trade just became a lot more concrete.

items.title: Dollar in major breakout — DXY target 103; EUR/USD to 1.11; gold flush to 3,800
items.idea: Dale Pinkert (TradeGateHub) calls the dollar's move a 'major breakout' with room to DXY 103. Implications: EUR/USD to 1.11 (from 1.15), GBP/USD under 1.30 (from 1.32), USD/CAD to 1.43. He views this as a bear market rally within the larger tariff-induced dollar breakdown. If DXY hits 103, he sees gold flushing to 3,800 (from current ~4,100+) and potentially 50 in silver. He prefers long AUD/USD at .67 among commodity currencies.
items.angle: The dollar is ripping simultaneously with the Iran peace deal reducing risk premium and Warsh removing the dovish Fed put. This is a toxic combo for dollar-denominated debt markets globally. EM borrowers with dollar debt are the quiet victim here.
items.tweetA: DXY in major breakout targeting 103. That means: EUR/USD to 1.11, GBP/USD under 1.30, gold potentially flushing to 3,800. This is happening as Warsh removes the dovish Fed put AND Iran reduces the risk premium. Dollar strength driven by fundamentals hits EM debt like a sledgehammer.
items.tweetB: Dale Pinkert: if DXY gets to 103, gold could flush to 3,800. Gold is already pulling back from resistance. This is the classic pattern — peace deal removes fear bid, strong dollar pressures the commodity bid. Buy the dip in gold becomes a lot more interesting at 3,800.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: DXY breakout chart with target; gold resistance; EUR/USD

items.title: S&P exhaustion gap filling; melt-up to 8,500-9,000 intact if April 6,300 low holds
items.idea: Dale Pinkert says the S&P gapped up on Iran peace deal Monday and is now 'filling the gap, which makes it look like an exhaustion gap.' Semis filled the gap in one day. However, his bull case is intact: melt-up to 8,500-9,000 if the April low of 6,300 holds. Russell 2000 correction coming to 260 (from ~285). SpaceX IPO: 'Hello bagholder.' TLT actually finished up a shade on Fed day — which Pinkert interprets as Warsh trying to 'save the bond market' (flatten the curve by hiking short rates while protecting long rates).
items.angle: The TLT-up-on-hawkish-Fed observation is the most counterintuitive signal in this note. If Warsh is deliberately trying to flatten/invert the curve by hiking short rates without blowing up long bonds, that's a very specific policy regime with big implications for duration positioning.
items.tweetA: TLT actually finished UP on Fed day while the front end was 'skyrocketing.' Pinkert's read: Warsh is trying to save the long bond market, not destroy it. Hike the front end, anchor the long end. If that's the game, the 30yr trade is not what it looks like.
items.tweetB: SPY is filling the Iran peace gap. Pinkert calls it an exhaustion gap. The melt-up target of 8,500-9,000 is still valid — but only if April 6,300 holds. The Russell 2000 correction is the tell: if small caps break to 260, the broadening narrative is over.

charts.desc: SPY gap fill chart; Russell 2000 chart; TLT chart; nat gas chart

items.title: Agriculture: 'BTR — Buy the Rain'; nat gas still embryonic bull market
items.idea: Dale Pinkert has been positioning for a buy in grains around the June solstice for weeks. He cites the CBOT floor trader saying 'during weather markets, you buy the rain breaks.' Corn 'bottomed two years ago' and is having a great week. Wheat is his solstice buy. Separately, nat gas has been his long since the lows — still considers it an 'embryonic bull market,' would only be wrong if it closes under $3. WTI oil he could see going below $70 but leans toward buying.
items.angle: Grains and nat gas as simultaneous bull cases is consistent with the 'Speculation Phase' inflation-commodity framework Capital Wars is describing. BTR is a seasonal pattern with a long history of success in weather markets.
items.tweetA: CBOT floor trader wisdom: 'BTR — Buy the Rain.' During summer weather markets, you buy the rain breaks in grains. Pinkert's been positioning for this since the June solstice. Corn bottomed two years ago. Wheat is now. The commodity cycle is real regardless of what the Fed does.
items.tweetB: Nat gas is Pinkert's 'embryonic bull market.' Wrong only if it closes under $3. WTI he leans toward buying below $70. Meanwhile oil is down 6 sessions on the Iran deal. These don't conflict — energy trades on fundamentals that outlast geopolitical noise.

items.title: US initial claims 226K — 4-week average at highest since December; labor market still solid
items.idea: US initial jobless claims came in at 226K (est. 225K; prev. 229K) — pulling back from last week's 230K which was the highest since late January. The 4-week average rose to 223.25K, the highest since December 2022, but still historically low. Continuing claims rose to 1.810mn, but the 4-week average of continuing claims remains lower y/y for the 19th consecutive week. For context, the 20-year pre-pandemic average for initial claims is 345K.
items.angle: The labor market is cooling gradually but not collapsing. The combo of a still-tight labor market + 4.2% CPI gives Warsh no reason to cut and every reason to lean hawkish. The soft-landing story isn't dead but the 'need to cut' story definitely is.
items.tweetA: Initial claims 226K. 4-wk average at the highest since December. Continuing claims at 1.810mn. Compared to the 20-year pre-pandemic average of 345K initial claims, this labor market is historically tight. Warsh has no cover to cut. The data gives him every reason to do exactly what he said.
items.tweetB: The 4-week average of continuing claims has been lower year-over-year for 19 consecutive weeks. Even as initial claims slowly tick up, the rate of deterioration has reversed. This isn't a labor market that's breaking — it's one that's plateaued. That's a problem for inflation doves.

charts.desc: Initial and continuing claims charts with historical context

items.title: a16z New Media one year in: 'go-direct as a service' with 1M X followers, 250K newsletter
items.idea: a16z's New Media division turned one year old. Their model: in-house creative (video, editorial), owned channels (1M X followers, 250K email subscribers, 1M podcast downloads/month), full-service team for portfolio companies, and a network leveraged with software and AI. Key thesis: 'To win you must go direct, and to go direct you must be interesting.' The Insider Media concept — where signal comes from people, not brands — is explicitly named as the model. They're now scaling fellowships for New Media practitioners.
items.angle: The a16z model proves something important: at top-tier VC scale, marketing is now a product, not a service. The question for portfolio companies is whether this is a moat (a16z gets the best founders because of this) or a commoditizing skill. The answer will determine how long the a16z edge lasts.
items.tweetA: a16z has 1M X followers, 250K email subscribers, and 1M podcast downloads/month — all in service of their portfolio companies. Their insight: 'signal comes from people, not brands.' They're not building a media company. They're building a signal amplification network. That's a different business.
items.tweetB: a16z calls their media arm 'go-direct as a service.' One year in, they've built a bigger media operation than most media companies. The real competitive advantage: they can guarantee distribution to portfolio founders — something no other investor can do. The 'Bear Hug' pitch just got a lot stronger.

items.title: Goldman exposes hidden crisis in US auto debt; EU ECB warns AI could trigger financial crisis
items.idea: Goldman Sachs flagged a building crisis in US subprime auto debt, with borrowers under increasing stress. Separately, Europe's Central Bank President warned about a possible AI-caused 'financial crisis.' The auto debt angle connects to the Goldman call: subprime auto defaults typically lead consumer credit cycles, and they're beginning to crack even with unemployment at 4.3%. The ECB's AI financial crisis warning is separate — likely about AI-driven market correlation and flash crash risk.
items.angle: Subprime auto debt is always the canary in the credit mine. Goldman flagging it means it's already in the data, not a forecast. The sequence: auto defaults → personal credit stress → retail spending pullback → earnings miss cycle. Watch $AXP $COF $SYF.
items.tweetA: Goldman just flagged a 'hidden crisis building in US auto debt.' This is the credit canary. Subprime auto defaults precede consumer credit stress which precedes retail spending pullback. With unemployment at 4.3% this is already happening. Watch $COF $SYF $AXP for the leading indicator.
items.tweetB: Europe's central bank president warned this week that AI could trigger a financial crisis. Goldman flagged auto debt stress. These aren't connected — but they share a theme: the risk points that weren't in anyone's model 12 months ago are now showing up in the data.

items.title: SPY fakeout below 21 EMA; 13 sessions with no new high — lower high forming?
items.idea: James Bulltard notes SPY gave a fakeout yesterday closing below the 21 EMA, then gapped back above it this morning. He sees a potential bull flag forming as long as SPY holds the 21 EMA. Key observation: the market hasn't made a new high in 13 sessions — this could be a lower high forming. Mag 7 has yet to participate meaningfully in the recovery. On Warsh: 'Does he really want to upset the President? I still have a hard time seeing him defy the person who put him there.'
items.angle: The 'Warsh won't actually hike because Trump' narrative is the bullish counter to Chamath's framework. If markets believe Warsh is hawkish in talk only, the yield curve won't price the full hawkish scenario. That credibility test is the defining macro question of H2 2026.
items.tweetA: 13 sessions with no new high in the S&P. Mag 7 has yet to join the recovery. And the question everyone is dancing around: will Warsh actually hike and risk the wrath of Trump? If the answer is no, bonds rally and the 'hawkish talk' narrative becomes a buying opportunity.
items.tweetB: SPY fakeout below the 21 EMA, immediately recaptured. This is what bull markets do. But 13 sessions with no new high while oil goes lower is a different signal than the AI-driven rip higher. The market is processing multiple things at once and the direction isn't clear.

items.title: Bruges was the original Internet Capital Market: bills of exchange, global trade, 50,000 people by 1300
items.idea: Peter Frankopan's essay on Bruges (for a new museum exhibition in Belgium) traces how the city became one of the most globally connected places on Earth between the 9th and 16th centuries — not through empire but through networks: seas, credit, relics, and connections. Key economic innovations in Bruges: Italian city-state bills of exchange and credit mechanisms that enabled massive trade scale, book transfers reducing risk, and civic courts with mercantile law expertise. By 1300, 50,000 people lived in Bruges — an extraordinary concentration for the era. It fell when Antwerp and Amsterdam learned from its model and outcompeted it.
items.angle: The Bruges story is a perfect mirror for today's crypto debate: the city that builds the best network infrastructure wins, until a more agile competitor copies the model and adds something better. Antwerp and Amsterdam did to Bruges what Ethereum may do to older chains — and what Solana is attempting to do to Ethereum.
items.tweetA: Peter Frankopan on Bruges: a city that created the credit and trade infrastructure of medieval Europe — bills of exchange, mercantile courts, global commodity flows — and reached 50,000 people by 1300. It fell when Amsterdam copied the model and added better port access. Every infrastructure monopoly has this story.
items.tweetB: Medieval Bruges invented a lot of what we now call financial infrastructure: letters of credit, book transfers, rapid dispute resolution, global correspondent banking. It worked until it didn't — harbour silted up, competitors appeared, and the network moved. ICM builders should read this essay.
